title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In a conversation with Jiles and Gwendolen, an elderly man named Manning recalls the history of Dr. Brent, who now lives in Crossby House. Manning discusses Miss Helen Kennedy, the sister of Dr. Kennedy, who returned to the area after marrying an officer from India. Manning recounts a malicious incident where someone cut the tennis net at the house into pieces. Following this, Miss Helen suffered an injury when she tripped on a rake in the middle of a dark driveway; the resulting wound on her foot would not heal, preventing her from dancing.

Jiles inquires about Jackie Averycliffe, a former employee of the law firm Feen and Worchman, whom Manning describes as a "low-class character" and a friend of Miss Helen. Manning denies that Averycliffe was responsible for the net, attributing the destruction to sheer stupidity or venting. Manning also mentions Walter Fein, a man who was devoted to Miss Helen but was not loved by her; Fein eventually went abroad and returned to become the head of his firm.

Later, Miss Marple joins Gwendolen and Jiles. Miss Marple reflects on the nature of unrequited love and jealousy, using the case of Mrs. Erskine and her husband to illustrate how unreturned affection can lead to mutual suffering. She cautions that feelings are not always reliable indicators of guilt, noting that people often act contrary to their established characters due to deep-seated motivations or blood ties. Miss Marple emphasizes the importance of the crime scene and suggests that finding the address of J.J. Avery, the owner of the Water Lily Carriage Company, would be a straightforward task.
